name: Patrol
description: An AI navigation behavior where an agent follows a defined route, schedule, or area loop while monitoring perception events.
design_notes: Patrol is a readable AI promise: the agent will cover space in a pattern until a perception_event interrupts it. The useful details are patrol_route_id, waypoint_index, wait timers, look arcs, route interruption, resume behavior, navmesh failure, and debug route visualization.
design_purpose: Give players observable movement patterns they can learn, avoid, distract, or interrupt.
Populate spaces with believable activity before chase, alarm, or combat states begin.
Let designers shape stealth and horror pacing through coverage gaps, pause points, and sightline timing.
player_fantasy: The player feels clever when they read a guard route, wait for the right opening, and move through danger without being caught.

parameters.name: patrol_route_id
parameters.description: Stable identifier for the route, area, or schedule assigned to the agent.

parameters.name: waypoint_index
parameters.description: Current route point used for movement, save/load restoration, and debug display.

parameters.name: waypoint_wait_ms
parameters.description: Waypoint dwell window before the agent advances to the next route point.

parameters.name: route_resume_policy
parameters.description: Rule used after chase, investigation, or scripted interruption ends.

parameters.name: path_replan_interval_ms
parameters.description: Minimum delay between path requests while moving along the patrol route.

edge_cases: A navmesh_query to the next waypoint fails because a door closed, a moving platform left, or dynamic blockers appeared.
After chase or investigation, the agent must decide whether to resume from nearest waypoint, saved waypoint_index, or a designer-authored fallback.
Multiple agents sharing patrol_route_id need offset timing or avoidance so they do not stack on the same waypoint.
Save/load during a wait timer must restore waypoint_index, remaining wait, look direction, and alert state consistently.
common_bugs: waypoint_index advances even when movement failed, causing the patrol to skip coverage-critical points.
Patrol resumes immediately after losing the player because investigation state clears before line-of-sight grace expires.
Look arcs snap at waypoints and unfairly detect the player during an animation/state synchronization pop.
Debug visualization shows route nodes but not the active navmesh_query, making blocked-path failures hard to diagnose.
balancing_notes: Long predictable routes reward planning; randomized routes need visible tells or they become perceived as unfair.
Coverage should intentionally leave timing windows, alternate tools, or route manipulation options for the player.
Patrol wait times and perception radius should be tuned together; long waits with wide sightlines can lock down a room completely.
accessibility_notes: Expose facing, suspicion, and route timing through readable visual cues as well as audio.
Avoid requiring players to memorize long invisible schedules without landmarks, map hints, or repeated route tells.

implementation_notes.unity: Use NavMeshAgent or grid navigation with patrol_route_id data stored outside the scene object; draw gizmos for route order, wait zones, sight arcs, and current navmesh_query failure reasons.
implementation_notes.godot: Use NavigationAgent2D/3D and store ai_state_id, patrol_route_id, waypoint_index, and wait timer on the agent; emit debug labels for resume_policy and blocked path results.
implementation_notes.unreal: Implement patrol as Behavior Tree or StateTree tasks with Blackboard keys for patrol_route_id, waypoint_index, and route_resume_policy; use AI Debugger to show active path and abort reason.
implementation_notes.web: Represent routes as graph nodes and run movement decisions on a fixed tick; persist waypoint_index and use deterministic decision order when multiple interruptions arrive in one frame.

relationships.reason: Patrol needs sight checks when route state can escalate into suspicion, chase, or combat.
Noise investigation gives patrol agents a believable interruption and return path after hearing events.
Patrol perception events can raise stealth alert state from idle to suspicious or searching.

example_games.title: Metal Gear Solid
example_games.note: Uses readable guard patrols as a stealth planning element.

example_games.title: Alien: Isolation
example_games.note: Uses patrol and search behavior to create pressure in horror spaces.

example_games.title: Dishonored
example_games.note: Uses guard patrol routes as stealth observation and timing pressure.
